topic: rant about NHS physio waiting lists

turns.content: man I need to vent about something because it's doing my head in
Go for it — what's going on?

NHS physio waiting lists bruv. 18 weeks. EIGHTEEN WEEKS. someone comes in with a shoulder injury and they're told yeah we'll see you in 4 and a half months. by that point the injury is either chronic or they've given up and just live with the pain. how is that acceptable

Yeah, it's a real problem. 18 weeks is the target, and a lot of trusts aren't even hitting that — some areas are looking at 6 months plus. By the time someone actually gets seen, the acute injury has often become a chronic condition that's harder and more expensive to treat. It's counterproductive from every angle.

exactly!! and the people who suffer the most are the ones who can't afford to go private you know. if you've got money you just pay 60 quid and see someone next week. if you haven't you wait and wait and your body compensates and then you've got 3 problems instead of 1

You're spot on. It creates a two-tier system where access to rehab depends on income, not clinical need. And those compensation patterns are a real thing — someone with a knee issue starts overloading the other side, develops hip pain, their gait changes, back pain follows. By the time physio actually starts, the picture is way more complex than it needed to be.

and the physios who ARE working in the NHS are burned out because they've got massive caseloads. you're seeing 12-15 patients a day with barely any time between appointments. can't give proper care like that. you're just putting out fires

It's a vicious cycle. High caseloads mean shorter appointments, less time for proper assessment and patient education, worse outcomes, more follow-ups needed, which adds to the waiting list even more. And then experienced physios leave for private practice or leave the profession entirely because the conditions are unsustainable, which makes the staffing shortage worse.

The CSP has been pushing for better funding and staffing ratios but it's a hard fight when the whole NHS is stretched.

tbh I think community physio clinics could help. like drop-in centres in local areas where people can get assessed and start basic rehab without going through the whole GP referral thing. early intervention would save the NHS bare money in the long run

That's actually a model that's been piloted in a few areas — first-contact physiotherapy where you can self-refer or go through a community hub rather than waiting for a GP referral. The data from those pilots has been really positive: earlier intervention, fewer unnecessary GP appointments, patients managed more efficiently.

Some PCNs (Primary Care Networks) have started employing first-contact physios. The challenge is scaling it up and getting the funding to make it widespread. But the evidence is there that it works.

see that's what I'm saying. the solution exists we just need people to actually fund it. it's mad that we know early physio saves money and improves outcomes but we still make people wait 18 weeks. makes no sense bruv

It really doesn't. It's one of those things where the short-term cost of investing in early access would pay for itself many times over in reduced long-term care costs, fewer surgeries, less chronic pain management, fewer people out of work. The economic case is solid — it's the political will and upfront funding that's missing.

Sounds like you care a lot about this stuff though. Is this something you're involved in professionally or personally?

bit of both tbh. I'm studying physio so I see it from the clinical side but I also just care about access you know. healthcare shouldn't depend on your bank balance. anyway rant over lol I just needed to get that off my chest. safe for listening

No need to apologise — it's a conversation worth having and you clearly know what you're talking about. The profession needs people who care about accessibility and equity, not just the clinical side. Hope the studying is going well, and feel free to rant any time.
